title: The Upstream Ruins

features.name: settlement-ghost
features.desc-on-touch: Foundations where houses stood. The estuary took everything except the pattern.

features.name: erased-archive
features.desc-on-touch: A shelf drowned by tides. You can feel what was recorded here before water took the words.

features.name: fence-remnant
features.desc-on-touch: What was kept in? What was kept out? The fence remembers the difference.

features.name: memory-crystalline
features.desc-on-touch: Salt and silt have formed a record. The tide writes in minerals what time erased in words.

features.name: elder-post
features.desc-on-touch: A post still standing. It has learned to let water pass through rather than resist.

inscription: the water remembers the settlements; the ruins remember the water; nothing is lost, only renamed by erosion

reverie.title: REVERIE — upstream where the current still held memory
narr: You walk the shallows where the settlement stood.
She points to a brick, half-buried. "Salt-marked," she says.
"How long?" The water is cool and rises to your knees.
"Longer than the archive has known its own address."
You both watch the current take the foundation deeper.
The estuary is patient. It waits for everything to dissolve.

file.name: Upstream_Settlement_Survey_1987_Partial.txt
file.note: A heritage record of dwellings no longer standing; water has amended the margins with new coordinates.

exits.label: back downstream to where salt and memory converge
further upstream to settlements predating the archive itself
into the side channels where forgotten tributaries remember their names
